meta.title: W210 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes E-Class W210 engine — M111, M112, M104, M113, OM604, OM605, OM611, OM612, OM613. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class W210 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W210 engine typically costs £1,200–£9,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M111 and OM604 four-cylinders sit at £1,200–£3,200, the M112 V6 at £2,200–£5,000, the OM613 diesel at £2,800–£5,000, and the M113 V8 at £4,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class W210 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112.940 V6 in the E 320 (post-1997) is our top recommendation — it's a proven, naturally aspirated six-cylinder that replaced the ageing M104 inline-six, with a documented reputation for exceeding 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. The OM613.961 3.2-litre common-rail diesel is also excellent.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class W210?

acceptedAnswer.text: Camshaft adjuster wear on the M112 V6 (E 240/280/320), valve cover oil leaks on the M113 V8 (E 430/55 AMG), wiring harness degradation and head gasket issues on the M104 inline-six (pre-facelift E 280/320), and injector wear on the OM611/612/613 common-rail diesels. The 5G-Tronic transmission conductor plate is also a common non-engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class W210 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you choose the right engine and maintain it. The M112 V6 and OM613 common-rail diesel are the generation's most dependable options. The M113 V8 is characterful and powerful, but oil leaks are a cost-of-ownership factor. The M104 inline-six is a classic but wiring harness degradation and head gasket failure are common. Pre-facelift diesels (OM604/605) should be avoided.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class W210 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM604/605 indirect-injection diesels (E 220 Diesel, E 250 Diesel) are the ones we'd advise against — they're noisy, underpowered, and increasingly hard to keep on the road. The pre-facelift M104 (E 280/320) is a characterful engine but wiring harness degradation makes it a risk unless already replaced. A neglected E 55 with V8 oil leaks is also to be avoided unless you have deep pockets.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W210 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle on the M112 V6 (camshaft adjusters), oil smell/visible seepage on the M113 V8, persistent electrical gremlins on the M104 (wiring harness), or loss of power on any diesel (injector/turbo) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class W210 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on the car's value. Against a well-kept £4,000 E 320, a £2,800–£5,000 M112 recon is 70–125% — often not viable. Against a £1,500 tired example, it's clearly a walk-away. For the V8, against a £5,000+ E 430/55, a £4,000–£9,000 recon is 80–180% — viable only on exceptional, low-mileage examples. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line: value the car first because the engine is often worth more than the car.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes E-Class W210 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance — camshaft adjuster wear is the limiting factor. The M113 V8 is similarly robust, with oil leaks the main cost, not bottom-end failure. The OM613 common-rail diesel is proven to exceed 250,000 miles on well-maintained examples. The M104 inline-six can reach 200,000+ miles but wiring harness degradation usually requires attention before then.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W210 and the S210?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are second-generation E-Class models sharing the same platform and engines. The W210 is the 4-door saloon (1995–2002, this page), while the S210 is the 5-door estate (1996–2003). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the S210 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E-Class W210 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., E 320) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-1999 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class W210 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation E-Class saloon — from the frugal OM604 diesel to the thundering 5.4-litre V8 in the E 55 AMG. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the facelift that brought common-rail diesel and the V6 revolution. Backed by 890+ real UK M112 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class W210 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W210 saloon specifically — including the 1999 facelift that brought common-rail diesel and the V6 revolution.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: M112: 90% confidence — 890 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; failure patterns well-documented. M113: 85% confidence — well-understood V8 with documented failure patterns. M111: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier. M104/M119: 70% confidence — older engines with pattern data. OM604/OM605: 70% confidence — older indirect-injection diesels. OM611/612/613: 75% confidence — common-rail diesels with emerging pattern data.

overview.intro: The W210 is the second generation of the Mercedes-Benz E-Class, introduced in 1995 as the successor to the W124. It brought a more aerodynamic, modern design with the distinctive four-headlamp face that defined 1990s Mercedes — and a raft of engineering improvements including a multi-link rear suspension. A major 1999 facelift revised more than 1,800 components, bringing the V6 M112 engines to replace the inline M104, and common-rail diesel technology with the OM611, OM612 and OM613 families.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W210 • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class W124 • Successor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class W211 • Sibling body styles in this generation: S210 estate (1996–2003).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M112 V6 engine family generated 890 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the E 320 and E 240 as these executive saloons enter classic-car territory.

items.quote: The 3.2-litre common-rail straight-six diesel offered 197 bhp and immense torque — and it was the most refined diesel in the range. Injector and turbo issues are its only real demands, but the common-rail system was a leap forward over the old indirect-injection diesels.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless torque and 40+ mpg from a six-cylinder.

items.quote: The 3.2-litre V6 replaced the ageing M104 inline-six, offering 217–221 bhp with smoother power delivery and better reliability. Camshaft adjuster wear is its only real weak point, but it's a well-understood fix.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 smoothness without V8 running costs.

items.quote: The 2.2-litre indirect-injection diesel is a relic of a bygone era — underpowered, noisy, and prone to injector and glow plug issues. The OM611 common-rail that replaced it was a revelation.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers who want a cheap classic and don't mind the noise.

items.quote: The 5.4-litre V8 delivers 354 bhp and a soundtrack to match, but it's an ageing AMG — valve cover oil leaks, timing chain wear, and the sheer cost of V8 parts make it the most expensive engine in the range to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: AMG collectors with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The V6 offers genuine performance (6.9s to 60mph) with significantly lower running costs than the V8 — and sits in the same used price bracket as the four-cylinder cars. It's the sweet spot of the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best all-round W210 experience.

items.quote: The 2.2-litre common-rail diesel is often dismissed as 'just the base diesel', but it offers 50+ mpg with none of the V8's fuel bills — and the common-rail system was a genuine step forward over the old OM604.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want economy without the agricultural feel of older diesels.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Supercharged Risk — budget for charger work.
Solid V6 — reliable upgrade over M104.
Best V6 — smooth, dependable, great value.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, timing chain wear
rows.ownershipVerdict: V8 Value — more power, similar cost.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, timing chain
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Icon — high power, high cost.

economicsRule.title: The W210 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£5,000 M112 recon against a £2,000–£6,000 W210 sits at 45–250% of the car's value — on sub-£4,000 examples, it often exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The M113 V8 recon at £4,000–£9,000 against a £3,000–£10,000 E 430/55 is 40–300% — viable only on exceptional examples. These are ageing cars; value the car first, because the engine is often worth more than the car on budget examples.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W210 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 200 (M111) or E 220 Diesel (OM604) — cheapest recon, simplest engines.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W210 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 55 AMG — V8 parts and specialist labour costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W210 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 320 V6 (M112.940) — V6 smoothness without V8 running costs.

problems.title: M112 V6 Camshaft Adjuster Wear
problems.affectedModels: W210 E 240, E 280 (V6), E 320 (V6) — all M112 variants.
problems.rootCause: The camshaft adjusters (variable valve timing) wear over time, leading to rattling on start-up, reduced power, and eventually check engine lights. Oil leaks from the camshaft covers are a secondary but common issue.
problems.recommendation: If your E 240, E 280 or E 320 rattles on cold start, don't delay — a £500–£1,000 adjuster replacement is a fraction of the £3,500+ engine replacement that follows if the adjuster fails entirely.

cta.label: Compare M112 engine prices for your W210 E 320 →

problems.title: M113 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.affectedModels: W210 E 430, E 55 AMG (all M113 variants).
problems.rootCause: The V8's valve cover gaskets degrade with age and heat cycling — oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds and causing burning smells.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage, have the gaskets replaced — a £600–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to engine damage.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your W210 E 430 →

problems.title: M104 Head Gasket & Wiring Harness Degradation
problems.rootCause: The M104 inline-six is a legendary engine, but two issues plague it with age: the head gasket can fail, allowing coolant and oil to mix; and the engine wiring harness insulation degrades, causing electrical gremlins.
problems.recommendation: On any pre-facelift M104-powered W210, have the wiring harness inspected and the cooling system pressure-tested — a £600–£1,200 investment is cheap insurance against a £3,500+ replacement.

cta.label: Compare M104 engine prices for your W210 E 320 →

problems.title: OM604/OM605 Indirect-Injection Diesel Ageing
problems.rootCause: These indirect-injection diesels are simple but ageing — injector wear, glow plug failure, and the pre-combustion chamber design make them noisy, smoky, and increasingly hard to keep on the road.
problems.recommendation: The OM611 common-rail diesel that replaced these engines was a massive improvement — if you're considering a pre-facelift diesel W210, budget for injector work, or look for a post-1998 CDI instead.

cta.label: Compare OM604 engine prices for your W210 E 220 Diesel →

replacementCosts.h2: W210 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the W210 saloon shares its platform with the S210 estate, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W210 saloon only (1995–2002). S210 estate (1996–2003) shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. Note the E 200 Kompressor was not sold in Germany and was primarily an export model.
